base_method_text:
{"arxiv_id":"2401.05932v3","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05932v3","title":"DiffDA: a Diffusion Model for Weather-scale Data Assimilation","authors":["Langwen Huang","Lukas Gianinazzi","Yuejiang Yu","Peter D. Dueben","Torsten Hoefler"],"published_date":"2024-01-11T14:11:12Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"The generation of initial conditions via accurate data assimilation is
crucial for weather forecasting and climate modeling. We propose DiffDA as a
denoising diffusion model capable of assimilating atmospheric variables using
predicted states and sparse observations. Acknowledging the similarity between
a weather forecast model and a denoising diffusion model dedicated to weather
applications, we adapt the pretrained GraphCast neural network as the backbone
of the diffusion model. Through experiments based on simulated observations
from the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, our method can produce assimilated global
atmospheric data consistent with observations at 0.25 deg (~30km) resolution
globally. This marks the highest resolution achieved by ML data assimilation
models. The experiments also show that the initial conditions assimilated from
sparse observations (less than 0.96% of gridded data) and 48-hour forecast can
be used for forecast models with a loss of lead time of at most 24 hours
compared to initial conditions from state-of-the-art data assimilation in ERA5.
This enables the application of the method to real-world applications, such as
creating reanalysis datasets with autoregressive data assimilation.","github_url":"https://github.com/spcl/DiffDA","main_contributions":"The paper introduces DiffDA, a novel machine learning data assimilation method that uses a denoising diffusion model to assimilate high-resolution weather data (0.25° resolution). It demonstrates data assimilation cycles by combining DiffDA with an ML weather forecasting model and uses a neural network backbone from a pre-trained ML forecast model (GraphCast), which can be easily upgraded.","methodology":"The method adapts a pre-trained GraphCast model as the backbone of a denoising diffusion model. It conditions the diffusion model with predicted atmospheric states and sparse column observations, using a soft mask and interpolated observations to strengthen the conditioning. The model is trained to approximate the reverse of the diffusion process, adding conditioning of predicted state and observation separately.","experimental_setup":"The experiments use the WeatherBench2 dataset (derived from ERA5 reanalysis) for training, validation, and testing. Simulated observations are created from ERA5 data. The method is evaluated using root mean square errors (RMSEs) against ERA5 data, comparing assimilated data and forecasts with different lead times. Experiments include single-step data assimilation, autoregressive data assimilation, and forecast on single-step assimilated data.","limitations":"The method currently only supports point measurements at a single time step as observations, excluding satellite imagery and radar soundings. It also lacks quality control of the input observation data, relying on simulated observations from the ERA5 dataset.","future_research_directions":"Future research directions include supporting broader types of observations and multiple time steps using loss-function-based conditioning, adding conditioning of satellite imagery directly into the diffusion model, and investigating quality control techniques for input observation data."}
add_method_texts:
{"arxiv_id":"2404.04057v3","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2404.04057v3","title":"Score identity Distillation: Exponentially Fast Distillation of
  Pretrained Diffusion Models for One-Step Generation","authors":["Mingyuan Zhou","Huangjie Zheng","Zhendong Wang","Mingzhang Yin","Hai Huang"],"published_date":"2024-04-05T12:30:19Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"We introduce Score identity Distillation (SiD), an innovative data-free
method that distills the generative capabilities of pretrained diffusion models
into a single-step generator. SiD not only facilitates an exponentially fast
reduction in Fr\'echet inception distance (FID) during distillation but also
approaches or even exceeds the FID performance of the original teacher
diffusion models. By reformulating forward diffusion processes as semi-implicit
distributions, we leverage three score-related identities to create an
innovative loss mechanism. This mechanism achieves rapid FID reduction by
training the generator using its own synthesized images, eliminating the need
for real data or reverse-diffusion-based generation, all accomplished within
significantly shortened generation time. Upon evaluation across four benchmark
datasets, the SiD algorithm demonstrates high iteration efficiency during
distillation and surpasses competing distillation approaches, whether they are
one-step or few-step, data-free, or dependent on training data, in terms of
generation quality. This achievement not only redefines the benchmarks for
efficiency and effectiveness in diffusion distillation but also in the broader
field of diffusion-based generation. The PyTorch implementation is available at
https://github.com/mingyuanzhou/SiD","github_url":"https://github.com/mingyuanzhou/SiD","main_contributions":"The paper introduces Score identity Distillation (SiD), a novel data-free method for distilling pretrained diffusion models into a single-step generator. SiD achieves an exponentially fast reduction in Fréchet inception distance (FID) during distillation and matches or exceeds the FID performance of the original teacher diffusion models, redefining efficiency and effectiveness benchmarks in diffusion distillation and diffusion-based generation.","methodology":"SiD reformulates forward diffusion processes as semi-implicit distributions and uses three score-related identities to create a loss mechanism. This mechanism trains the generator using its own synthesized images, eliminating the need for real data or reverse-diffusion-based generation. The method approximates a model-based score-matching loss, a variant of Fisher divergence, using Monte Carlo estimation techniques.","experimental_setup":"The SiD algorithm was evaluated on four benchmark datasets: CIFAR-10 32x32, ImageNet 64x64, FFHQ 64x64, and AFHQv2 64x64. The single-step generator was trained using VP-EDM checkpoints as the teacher diffusion models. Performance was measured using Fréchet inception distance (FID), Precision, and Recall.","limitations":"SiD requires managing three networks (pretrained score network, generator score network, and generator) during distillation, increasing memory demands compared to traditional diffusion model training. It also exhibits increased memory allocation and iteration time compared to Diff-Instruct, due to the computation of score gradients.","future_research_directions":"Future research could explore reducing the memory footprint of the additional networks by employing LoRA for both the generator score network and the generator. Additionally, exploring advanced strategies such as progressively increasing the batch size, annealing the learning rate, and implementing gradient clipping could be investigated."}
